entry.title: AI-Powered Field Service Management in 2602
entry.content:
Welcome to SAP Field Service Management 2602 – a release designed to help field service teams plan and execute work with more speed, clarity, and confidence. As always, we have summarized the key highlights and included demo videos for you to explore.

SAP Field Service Management 2602 continues our investment in AI-driven decision support, dispatcher productivity, and mobile execution. Across scheduling, insights, reservations, maps, crews, and partner collaboration, this release reduces friction in the daily work of planning and execution – so teams spend less time navigating the system and more time completing work.

For the complete list of all features in the 2602 release, click here.

Key Highlights in SAP Field Service Management 2602

  • AI Best Matching Technician (BMT) and policy explanations – understand technician ranking with business-friendly AI explanations.
  • Technician facing AI – AI Activity Summary and AI Technician Briefings.
  • New UI for AI Insights – redesigned insights consumption with dedicated cards and tabs.
  • Crews – streamline crew planning and dispatch actions for greater flexibility.
  • Service Map – ArcGIS Online lasso selection, rearrange scheduling on map, regional boundary capture, and more.
  • Crowd Service – time and material journal timesheets in the partner portal.
  • PM integration – integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, asset management (scope item: 7Z2).

AI-Driven Scheduling and Dispatching

Check out the highlights in the demo video below:


AI Best Matching Technician (BMT) explanations

Dispatchers need to assign the right technician quickly – but they also need to trust and explain the decision. As scheduling becomes more policy-driven, the “why” behind ranking matters just as much as the recommendation itself.

In 2602, Best Matching Technician explanations provide business-friendly reasoning for technician ranking. Explanations summarize relevant policy objectives, rules, and scoring drivers – enabling dispatchers to validate outcomes without deep technical analysis.

This supports faster decision-making, smoother exception handling, and stronger trust in AI-assisted scheduling. Learn more in the release documentation.

New UI for AI Insights

Insights are most valuable when they are easy to absorb in the moment. Dispatchers and back-office users need to scan context quickly – especially when balancing high-volume work with urgent changes.

In 2602, we redesigned the AI Insights user interface to improve readability and navigation. Each section is presented in dedicated cards, with tabs that make it easier to move between context, history, and summaries. This helps users focus on the information that matters most without hunting through dense layouts.

The experience is faster, clearer, and easier to trust – especially when insights are part of everyday workflows. Explore the feature in SAP Help Portal.

 

Quick view for reservations

Reservations are essential for planning – training, safety briefings, meetings, and time blocks that shape technician availability. Yet dispatchers often lose time navigating into reservation details just to understand what a time block represents.

In 2602, dispatchers can open a quick view for reservations directly from the dispatching board. The quick view provides an overview of key reservation details such as type, address, and timeline.

This makes reservation constraints easier to interpret during scheduling, while reducing unnecessary clicks. See the details here.

 

Multi-select drag and drop for reservations

Schedule changes often require moving more than one reservation at a time. When dispatchers must adjust reservations one-by-one, the workflow slows down.

With 2602, dispatchers can multi-select reservations and drag and drop them together to a different timeframe or resource. This makes large schedule adjustments feel lightweight – even when many time blocks need to move together.

This means fewer repetitive actions, lower risk of oversight, and faster schedule changes. Full notes are available in the documentation.

 

Activity-based highlighting on the planning board for Activity UDFs

Dispatchers often rely on custom fields to reflect operational reality – multi-person jobs, special certifications, or internal workflow flags. Without strong visual cues, recognizing patterns across scheduled work can be difficult on busy boards.

In 2602, activity-based highlighting on the planning board is extended to support Activity UDF values. Dispatchers can highlight activities based on relevant custom field values and more easily spot relationships and dependencies across the schedule.

This gives planners a faster way to scan the board and connect the dots – without opening every activity. Learn more here.

 

Mobile Field Service

Check out the highlights in the demo video below:


AI Technician Briefing Summary

Technicians often start the day without a clear overview of what they have assigned, where they need to go, and what parts/tools they should bring. That lack of preparation can impact first-time fix and create avoidable return visits.

In 2602, AI Technician Briefing can generate a daily briefing that summarizes the day’s activities and includes helpful information such as tools required and location context. It can be generated through business rules and sent by email, giving technicians a clearer start before the shift begins.

Better preparation up front helps reduce surprises in the field and supports stronger first-time fix performance. For setup and prerequisites, refer to the product documentation.

 

AI Activity Summary

Writing activity summaries is necessary – but it is time-consuming, and quality can vary widely. That makes it harder for customers and colleagues to quickly identify the key facts and next steps.

In 2602, AI Activity Summary generates a consistent activity summary that technicians can quickly review and adjust (if needed). This reduces time spent documenting work while improving the clarity and consistency of what gets recorded.

Technicians wrap up faster, and teams get cleaner handoffs and more reliable service history. Learn more here.

 

Quick action bar

Technicians lose time navigating menus to complete common tasks – logging effort, mileage, materials, expenses, and handover or takeover steps. These interruptions add friction during execution.

In 2602, a configurable quick action bar provides one-click access to the most commonly used actions This keeps mobile work focused and reduces unnecessary navigation.

The workflow accelerates – fewer taps, faster completion, and less disruption throughout the day. See the details here.

 

Filter by person responsible list

Selecting the right colleague shouldn’t require scrolling through long lists – especially when technicians need to move quickly while locking effort or collaborating with supporting resources.

In 2602, the responsible list can be prefiltered to show only people connected to the selected activity, such as the same team or supporting technicians.

That keeps data entry fast and accurate, and makes multi-person jobs with frequent handoffs easier to manage.  Learn more in the documentation.

 

Service Map, Crews, and Crowd Service

Check out the highlights in the demo video below:


Service map – lasso selection for multiple work orders (ArcGIS Online)

In high-volume scenarios, dispatchers often need to take action on clusters of work in a geographic area. Without bulk selection, each work order must be handled individually, slowing response when speed matters.

With lasso selection in the service map for ArcGIS Online, dispatchers can select multiple work orders with GIS context and act on them together. This supports faster planning and more efficient response during localized surges, outages, or time-critical events.

It’s a practical improvement that makes geo-based work far more scalable. See the details here.

 

Service map – quick planning (rearrange) directly on the map

When priorities shift, planners need a fast way to rebalance schedules while keeping geography in view.

In 2602, rearrange schedule on the map enables planners to automatically reorganize technician schedules based on pre-defined scheduling policies– directly from the map context.

This helps keep plans realistic as conditions change, while reducing travel and improving responsiveness. See more here.  

 

Service map – capture and update region polygon details

Region boundaries were previously maintained outside of the Service Map UI, which increases overhead when planners need to maintain territories or coverage areas.

In 2602, planners can capture or update region polygon details directly on the service maps using lasso selection. This improves situational awareness and eliminates dependency on additional tools.

Territory updates become easier to maintain, and geographic context stays closer to the planning workflow. Full configuration notes are available in the documentation.

 

Streamline planning and dispatch process capabilities for crews

Crew-based operations require flexible planning actions – assigning, reassigning, and planning groups of work in ways that match real execution models.

In 2602, we streamline crew planning by enabling key planning actions such as “Assign To,” “Reassign To,” and “Duplicate For,” and by allowing planning of groups to crews.

This expands what teams can do with crews on the dispatching board and reduces the need for workaround steps. Learn more in the release documentation.

 

Crowd Service – time and material journal timesheets in partner portal

Contractors and partners need a consistent way to capture and manage time efforts, expenses, mileage, and materials – not only through mobile tools.

In 2602, the partner portal includes an integrated time and material journal so partners can manage timesheets and related entries in one place.

This supports cleaner collaboration with external resources and improves predictability from execution through billing. Explore the feature in SAP Help Portal.

 

Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Check out the highlights in the demo video below:


Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Asset Management (scope item: 7Z2)

Maintenance execution depends on strong coordination between back-office planning and field execution. Without out-of-the-box support, many Cloud ERP customers relied on project-based integrations.

In 2602, integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Asset Management (scope item: 7Z2) supports maintenance order scenarios with synchronization between asset maintenance planning and field service execution.

It provides a more connected experience across planning, scheduling, dispatch, mobile execution, and closure. See the details here.

 

Final Thoughts

SAP Field Service Management 2602 strengthens day-to-day execution for dispatchers, planners, and technicians.

Explainable AI BMT ranking, a redesigned AI insights experience, and faster reservation handling make planning more confident and less manual. Mobile improvements reduce administrative overhead and keep technicians focused with AI briefings, summaries, and faster actions. Service map and crew enhancements make high-volume planning more scalable, while partner portal timesheets and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition integration support cleaner end-to-end execution across internal and external teams.

We look forward to your feedback through Influence, the SAP Community, and upcoming webinars.

Check out other recent SAP Field Service Management releases.

entry.title: Your mission briefing has arrived: WalkMe Realize 2026
entry.content:
Most organizations implementing AI right now are running into the same problem: the technology works, but the people using it aren't seeing the benefit yet. If that sounds familiar, you're not alone. And you're probably asking the right questions. This post is about an event designed to help answer them.

On March 18, 2026, WalkMe is activating its flagship virtual event: Realize 2026. This isn't a webinar. It's a cinematic two-hour operation, a mission briefing for leaders driving AI adoption inside their organizations.

 

What to expect: A different kind of virtual event

Forget sprawling agendas and back-to-back panels. Realize 2026 is built around one clear objective: AI adoption that actually works. Not as a pilot program, but at enterprise scale, across your entire workforce.

Every operative starts with Mission: Adoption, a keynote featuring WalkMe CEO Dan Adika, product leaders, customers from Mondelēz International and Schneider Electric, and Daniel Newman, CEO of The Futurum Group. They'll get into why AI only delivers value when employees can actually use it, with real examples, live demos, and honest customer truth. From there, you dive into specialized pro-tip tracks featuring experts from ServiceNow, CHRISTUS Health, and WalkMe covering visibility, experience design, and adoption strategies at scale.

Leave with this

Whether you're leading AI adoption across a global enterprise or just trying to get traction in your corner of it, this is built for you. You'll walk away with:

  • A clear picture of what separates organizations seeing real AI adoption from those still stuck in pilot mode.
  • Real intel from customers on how they've driven success.
  • An early look at new WalkMe capabilities designed to close the gap between AI investment and actual employee usage.
  • Pro-tip sessions with action plans you can put to work right away.

New to WalkMe? Start here.

Never used WalkMe before? Good time to show up. Realize 2026 is designed so that whether you're a seasoned WalkMe practitioner or exploring it for the first time, you'll walk away with something concrete. If you're trying to figure out whether a digital adoption platform is right for your organization or where WalkMe fits in your SAP ecosystem, this is the right room.

Accept the mission

👉[Register for Realize 2026] 👈

Have questions about WalkMe, digital adoption, or AI rollout challenges in your organization? Drop them in the comments below. I'd love to hear what you're working through.

entry.title: Quick Financial Plan - Key Updates in SAP Commercial Project Management S/4 HANA 2025 FSP01 release
entry.content:
The S/4 HANA 2025 FSP01 release brings significant enhancements to Quick Financial Plan (QFP) capabilities within SAP S/4 HANA Commercial Project Management (CPM), reinforcing SAP`s commitment to smarter, faster and more integrated financial planning. In this blog, we explore the key innovations delivered in the S/4 HANA 2025 FSP01 release and how they help in streamlining the planning process using QFP`s capability as a comprehensive financial planning component in CPM.

Enhanced Distribution with addition of Rate (Cost) and Rate (Revenue) fields for Quantity-Driven Resource Types

The Distribute functionality has been enhanced for quantity-driven resource types. The pop-up dialog now allows planners to enter Rate (Cost) and Rate (Revenue) in addition along with Quantity. 

Fig 1: Rate (Cost) and Rate (Revenue) added for Quantity-driven Resource Types in QFP

 The system automatically applies the Rate across all planning periods along with the allocated Quantities. Financial values like Cost (Transaction), Cost (Plan), Revenue (Transaction), Revenue (Plan) are also calculated by the system, thereby eliminating manual intervention.

Fig 2: Rate automatically distributed across all periods in Distribution

Addition of Import Functionality

The QFP page for planning now includes a dedicated Import button. Planning data can be brought in from associated Project System projects into Commercial Projects.

Fig 3: Import button added in QFP

Upon selecting Import Data under Import button, the system initiates the import of data from PS project. The import process is executed based on the import method and import strategies configured in SPRO. During execution, the system automatically determines the mapping between the PS elements and the corresponding CPM structure. The progress of the import can be monitored by clicking the Refresh button. By expanding the page header, the current status of the import can be viewed in the Import Status indicator.Fig 4: Check status of Import using the Import Status indicator

Once the Import Status indicator turns green, the imported data can be immediately accessed in the QPF page. The system updates the data in real time, so there is no need to reload the application to view the imported data. Fig 5: Select structure element from Financial Summary to view Import results

The Import Log can be reviewed to access details of the imported data, including the import methods and strategies applied. Additionally, there is an option to simulate the import using Simulate Data function and review the details in Simulate Log. This allows verification of import process before executing it in live environment.

Fig 6: Additional functionalities available under Import button in QFP

Output Based Planning

Output Based planning is now enabled on QFP for monthly and fiscal breakdown scenarios. Upon selecting the Plan button in Financial Plan, the system displays the Output Based planning option. 

Fig 7: Output Based Planning added under Plan in Financial Plan

QFP allows output quantity to be planned to statistical key figure in Output Based planning. In addition, the standard options to Distribute and Delete the entries are present, similar to any other financial plan.Fig 8: Select statistical key figure to link output quantities in QFP

Once the output quantity is planned, the corresponding Input Quantity, Cost needed to achieve the planned outputs can be planned in Input Resource Plan. The standard options of Calculate Cost, Delete and Distribute are available in the input plan. Additionally, a new Calculate quantity button has been introduced to compute the input quantity based on Productivity (Plan) and planned output quantity. Once the input plan is entered, the system automatically populates the Unit price and Productivity fields in the output plan, streamlining the planning process and reducing manual effort. Fig 9: Define Input Resource Plan values to achieve the output quantities in QFP

Cash Flow Planning

Cash Flow planning is now embedded within QFP for monthly and fiscal breakdown scenarios, giving organizations a clear view of the incoming and outgoing payments of projects based on the financial plan. This makes it easier to manage resources efficiently and provides a comprehensive view of project financial health. 

Consider the below monthly planning:

Fig 10: Monthly planning to review Cash Flow

In order to access Cash Flow in QFP, select Cash Flow under Plan in Financial Plan. When the QFP Page opens, a new button Cash Flow is displayed. Fig 11: Cash Flow added under Plan in Financial Plan

Fig 12: Cash Flow button added in QFP

Once the Cash Flow button is executed, it triggers the valuation strategy and method associated to the resource type in planning. The incoming and outgoing payment values are then copied to the fields present in the valuation method configured in SPRO.

Fig 13: Select structure element from Financial Summary to view Cash Flow results

 QFP also allows to adjust the timings of payments using period shifts. By specifying values in period shift field, the system can postpone incoming or outgoing payments to a later date. Fig 14: Period shift in Cash Flow

In the above example the period shift for March is set to 31 and Cash Flow is executed again. The payments are shifted by 31 days i.e. to April. This feature helps prevent timing mismatches and ensures that cash flow projections remain accurate and up to date.

Fig 15: Select structure element from Financial Summary to view Period Shift results

Additional information

For more details, you may refer below links:

  • SAP CPM S/4 HANA 2025 FSP01 release
  • What`s New Viewer - SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

entry.title: From HANA Studio to HANA Cloud: The Pain Points Nobody Warns You About
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  TECHNICAL GUIDE |  SAP HANA CLOUD MIGRATION

From HANA Studio to HANA Cloud: The Pain Points Nobody Warns You About

A practitioner's guide to the friction points that slow down on-premise to HANA Cloud migrations — covering tooling gaps, scripting compatibility, and security model changes that will catch your team off guard.

By Deepa Dorairaj   | SAP AI Solution Architect  |  HANA Data Engineering  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Migrating from SAP HANA Studio on-premise to HANA Cloud is not a lift-and-shift. The tooling changes, the security model changes, and a significant portion of your existing stored procedures will need rework before they run. This article covers the three areas where teams consistently underestimate the effort — and exactly what to watch out for in each.

The Migration Gap Nobody Talks About

SAP HANA Cloud is the right destination for most organizations still running on-premise HANA landscapes. The scalability, the managed infrastructure, the BTP integration story — the strategic case is clear. What's less clear, until you're in the middle of it, is how much the day-to-day operational reality changes between HANA Studio on-premise and HANA Cloud Central.

Teams that have spent years building muscle memory in HANA Studio — navigating schemas, editing calculation views graphically, managing users through a familiar interface, writing stored procedures against a predictable execution context — will find that the cloud environment is not a 1:1 replacement. It is a different tool, with different constraints, different patterns, and different failure modes.

This article is not a migration how-to. It is a field guide to the specific pain points that will slow your team down, based on direct experience with this transition. The goal is to give you the information early enough to plan for it rather than discover it mid-project.

PAIN POINT 1 OF 3 

HANA Studio Features That Don't Exist in HANA Cloud Central

The Schema Browser and Object Navigation Gap

HANA Studio's system and schema browser is one of its most underappreciated features. Developers and architects who have used it for years navigate schema structures, object dependencies, and table definitions almost by instinct — expanding trees, right-clicking to view DDL, drilling into column definitions without leaving their context.

HANA Cloud Central's database explorer is capable, but the navigation paradigm is meaningfully different. The object hierarchy is presented differently, context menus behave differently, and the workflow for tasks as simple as viewing a table's full column list or checking an index definition requires more steps than Studio users are accustomed to. This sounds minor until you factor in the cumulative friction across a team of developers doing this hundreds of times a day.

Watch out: Teams consistently underestimate the productivity dip in the first 4-6 weeks after cutover. Budget time for your team to rebuild navigation muscle memory in the new tool. What takes 30 seconds in Studio may take 3 minutes in Cloud Central until the team is fluent.

Specific navigational differences to prepare for:

  • Schema-level object counts and quick overviews that were visible at a glance in Studio require explicit queries or additional clicks in Cloud Central.
  • The right-click 'Open DDL' shortcut that Studio developers rely on is not directly replicated — you will need to query SYS.OBJECTS or use the SQL console to retrieve DDL for many object types.
  • Cross-schema object browsing that was seamless in Studio requires explicit privilege grants to be visible in Cloud Central's explorer, which surfaces privilege gaps earlier but creates friction during initial setup.

The Graphical Calculation View Editor

This one causes more frustration than almost any other tooling difference. Teams that built complex calculation views in HANA Studio's graphical editor — star schemas, multi-join hierarchies, calculated columns, input parameters — are accustomed to a specific visual workflow that has been refined over many versions of the on-premise product.

The calculation view editor in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), which replaces the Studio graphical editor for HANA Cloud, is architecturally different. It is web-based, operates through HDI containers rather than directly against the database schema, and has a different set of supported node types and behaviors.

Critical: Calculation views that reference classic schema objects directly cannot be opened in the BAS graphical editor without first migrating them to HDI container-based deployment. This is not a minor adjustment — it affects the entire deployment and change management workflow for your calculation view landscape.

What this means in practice:

  • Views that were deployed and edited directly against the HANA schema in Studio must be converted to HDI artifacts (.hdbcalculationview files) before they can be managed in the Cloud toolchain.
  • The visual editor in BAS handles most standard patterns well but has gaps for certain advanced features that Studio supported — particularly around some legacy join types and specific aggregation node configurations.
  • Teams using vibe coding approaches for script conversion will find calculation view migration the hardest area to automate reliably — the graphical representation does not translate cleanly to text-based artifacts without manual review.

Practical recommendation: Before cutover, audit your calculation view landscape and categorize views by complexity. Simple star schema views migrate cleanly. Complex multi-join hierarchies with custom aggregation behaviors need hands-on migration effort, not automated conversion.

 

Watch Out For

Why It Matters

Schema browser navigation

Muscle memory built in Studio does not transfer — plan for a productivity dip and explicit team training time

DDL viewing workflow

Right-click DDL access is not replicated — teams need to learn alternative query-based approaches

Calculation view editor

Studio graphical editor → BAS editor is not a direct replacement; HDI container migration required first

Advanced view features

Some Studio-supported node types and behaviors have gaps in BAS — audit before migrating complex views

PAIN POINT 2 OF 3 

Stored Procedure and Scripting Compatibility Issues

Deprecated Syntax That Silently Worked On-Premise

HANA Cloud runs on a more recent version of the HANA engine than most on-premise landscapes. Syntax and behaviors that were technically deprecated in earlier HANA versions but continued working on-premise — because the on-premise version hadn't yet enforced the deprecation — will fail in HANA Cloud with no grace period.

The challenge is that these failures are not always obvious during planning. A stored procedure that has run reliably for three years on-premise, never touched because it works, may fail immediately in HANA Cloud because it uses syntax that was deprecated in HANA 2.0 SPS04 and the on-premise landscape never enforced the removal.

The most common deprecated patterns we encountered: EXEC statements used where EXECUTE IMMEDIATE is the correct modern syntax, certain FOR loop constructs that behave differently in the Cloud SQL engine, and ARRAY handling patterns that were partially supported on-premise but have stricter enforcement in Cloud.

The vibe coding approach to manual script conversion helps significantly here — using AI-assisted code review to scan procedure bodies for known deprecated patterns before migration is faster than manual review at scale. But it requires a human review pass on the output because the conversion suggestions are not always context-aware enough to handle complex procedure logic correctly.

Implicit Type Conversion Differences

HANA Cloud is stricter about implicit type conversions than on-premise HANA in several specific scenarios. Code that relied on HANA silently casting between numeric types, or between date and string types in certain contexts, will produce errors or subtly different results in Cloud.

-- On-premise: this worked with implicit STRING to DATE cast

-- HANA Cloud: requires explicit CAST or TO_DATE conversion

WHERE change_date > '2024-01-01'

-- HANA Cloud safe version:

WHERE change_date > TO_DATE('2024-01-01', 'YYYY-MM-DD')

The more dangerous scenario is numeric type conversion. HANA Cloud's stricter enforcement of arithmetic precision in certain contexts means that calculations which produced correct results on-premise may produce different results in Cloud — not errors, but silently wrong numbers. This is harder to catch in testing because the procedure runs successfully.

Watch out: Implicit conversion issues in financial calculations are particularly risky. Any procedure that performs arithmetic across mixed numeric types (INTEGER, DECIMAL, DOUBLE) should be explicitly reviewed and tested against known expected outputs before cutover.

Procedure Privileges and Execution Context Changes

On-premise HANA procedures run with a well-understood privilege model that many teams have not had to think about deeply — the procedure owner's privileges, or explicitly granted execution rights, determine what the procedure can access. HANA Cloud introduces execution context behaviors that are different in the HDI container model and stricter about cross-container and cross-schema access.

Procedures that accessed objects in multiple schemas by relying on the executing user's broad schema privileges will break in HANA Cloud if those cross-schema access patterns are not explicitly replicated through the new privilege model.

  • DEFINER vs INVOKER rights behavior differs between on-premise and Cloud in specific scenarios — procedures that relied on implicit DEFINER rights behavior need explicit review.
  • Cross-schema procedure calls that worked on-premise through broad user grants will require explicit synonym or privilege configuration in the Cloud environment.
  • Procedures that called other procedures across schema boundaries need the full dependency chain mapped before migration — missing one intermediate privilege breaks the entire call chain.

 

Watch Out For

Why It Matters

Deprecated syntax

Procedures that ran for years on-premise may fail immediately in Cloud — scan all procedure bodies before migration

Implicit type conversions

Stricter enforcement means silent wrong results, not always errors — test financial calculations against known outputs

FOR loop and EXEC patterns

Common on-premise scripting patterns have changed behavior or stricter enforcement in HANA Cloud SQL engine

Cross-schema procedure calls

Privilege chains that worked on-premise need explicit remapping in the Cloud privilege model

PAIN POINT 3 OF 3 

Security and User Management Changes

The Role and Privilege Model Is Fundamentally Different

On-premise HANA security administration through HANA Studio is direct and familiar — create users, assign roles, grant privileges, done. The privilege model is granular but the tooling is transparent. What you see in the security editor is what exists in the database.

HANA Cloud introduces a layered security model that operates differently depending on whether you are working in the HDI container context, the plain schema context, or through BTP's identity and access management layer. Teams that migrate assuming the privilege model works the same way will create security configurations that appear to work in testing but fail in production under specific access patterns.

The most common mistake: Granting broad schema-level privileges in HANA Cloud the same way they were granted on-premise, then discovering that certain operations still fail because the HDI container security layer has separate privilege requirements that aren't covered by schema-level grants.

HDI Containers Replacing Classic Schema-Based Access

If your on-premise landscape uses classic schema-based deployment — objects created directly in database schemas, accessed directly by users with schema privileges — the HDI container model is a significant conceptual shift, not just a technical one.

In HDI containers, database objects are owned by the container's technical user, not by the schema owner or the deploying user. Access to those objects by application users goes through a specific HDI access role pattern that is different from the direct privilege grants your team is used to managing.

  • Users and roles that worked on-premise cannot be directly imported into HANA Cloud — the privilege model differences mean a fresh role design is almost always necessary.
  • Application users that previously connected directly to the HANA schema need to be restructured to go through the appropriate HDI access pattern or plain schema equivalent in Cloud.
  • The tooling for managing HDI container privileges is in BTP Cockpit and HANA Cloud Central, not in a single location — teams need to understand which layer manages which aspect of the privilege model.

Practical recommendation: Before migration, document every user, role, and privilege assignment in your on-premise landscape. Do not attempt a direct import or recreation — use that documentation as a reference to design a new privilege model that fits the HANA Cloud architecture. The effort is significant but unavoidable.

SSO and Identity Provider Configuration

On-premise HANA landscapes often use database-native user authentication or LDAP integration managed directly in the HANA system. HANA Cloud on BTP uses SAML-based SSO through BTP's Identity Authentication Service (IAS) or a connected corporate identity provider.

Teams that relied on database-native authentication for both human users and technical system users will find that HANA Cloud's integration with BTP's identity layer requires a different configuration approach for each user type. Technical users — RFC connections, ETL tool connections, reporting tool connections — often cannot use SAML-based SSO and require specific certificate or password-based authentication configuration that is handled differently in Cloud.

Watch out: Every system that connects to your on-premise HANA — ETL tools, reporting platforms, RFC connections, middleware — needs its authentication method reviewed against HANA Cloud's supported patterns before cutover. This is frequently the last item tested and the most common cause of post-cutover incidents.

Loss of OS-Level Access

On-premise HANA gives administrators OS-level access to the underlying server — the ability to check file system paths, review trace files directly, restart services, and perform low-level diagnostics that go beyond what the database interface exposes.

HANA Cloud is a managed service. OS-level access does not exist. All diagnostics and administration must go through HANA Cloud Central, the SQL console, or the available monitoring views. Teams that have built operational runbooks around OS-level access — checking trace file directories, manually managing backup files, scripting OS-level health checks — need to rebuild those runbooks entirely against the Cloud-native toolset.

  • Trace file access moves to HANA Cloud Central's diagnostic tools — the content is the same but the access method is completely different.
  • Backup and recovery operations that were managed through OS-level scripts need to be rebuilt using HANA Cloud's backup catalog and BTP-native tooling.
  • Custom OS-level monitoring scripts used by your operations team will not function and need Cloud-native equivalents built before cutover.

 

Watch Out For

Why It Matters

Role and privilege model

On-premise privilege assignments cannot be directly migrated — design a new model for Cloud from scratch

HDI container access

Classic schema-level grants do not cover HDI container object access — separate privilege configuration required

SSO configuration

Every connecting system needs authentication method reviewed against Cloud's supported patterns

OS-level access

All OS-level runbooks and monitoring scripts must be rebuilt using HANA Cloud Central and Cloud-native tools

What This Means for Your Migration Plan

The three areas covered in this article — tooling gaps, scripting compatibility, and security model changes — are the ones most consistently underestimated in HANA Cloud migration projects. They share a common characteristic: they are invisible during the planning phase because the on-premise system works fine, and they surface as a cluster of friction points in the first weeks after cutover.

The teams that navigate this transition most successfully do two things differently. First, they run a structured pre-migration audit — cataloging calculation views by complexity, scanning stored procedures for deprecated patterns, and mapping every privilege assignment before touching the Cloud environment. Second, they treat the first month in Cloud Central as a productivity investment, not a productivity loss — explicitly budgeting time for the team to build new tooling fluency before expecting output at on-premise velocity.

The migration is worth doing. HANA Cloud's operational model, scalability, and BTP integration story are genuinely better than maintaining on-premise infrastructure. But it is not a lift-and-shift, and approaching it as one is the single most reliable way to turn a manageable transition into a painful one.

If your team is in the planning phase of this migration and you want to discuss specific aspects of the audit process or privilege model redesign, drop a comment below. These are the conversations worth having before cutover, not after.

entry.title: How to configure Single Sign-On (SSO) between S/4HANA Cloud and BTP
entry.content:
I have subscribed to SAP Business Technology Platform and developed an application using SAP Business Application Studio. The application was successfully deployed to the Cloud Foundry environment.

After that, I created a custom app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud and assigned the final application URL (obtained from BAS) to the app so that the application can be launched directly from the S/4HANA Cloud system.

Currently, the application can be executed successfully from S/4HANA Cloud. However, users are required to enter BTP credentials every time they access the application. Since business users of the S/4HANA Cloud system are not able to provide BTP credentials for each execution, this approach is not convenient for them.

I found that Single Sign-On (SSO) needs to be configured between S/4HANA Cloud and BTP to avoid repeated authentication.

Could you please share any blogs, reference guides, or official documentation that explain how to configure SSO between these two systems?

Best Regards,

Khaing Zin

entry.title: Synchronized scrolling for two or more tables
entry.content: In SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) stories, when two tables are placed side-by-side (for example, to compare actual data against budgets from different models), each table maintains its own scroll bar. This prevents business users from keeping rows aligned when scrolling vertically or columns aligned when scrolling horizontally. We need a function to link or lock the scrolling of two widgets and ensure data alignment during analysis.

entry.title: Re: SAP note required by itself? Question about SAP Suppo...
entry.content: For me, they indicate no external dependencies rather than a bug. As you might be knowing, SAP uses the report SCWN_PREREQUISITE_CALC_SWI to determine prerequisite notes. The target note itself is included in the hierarchy tree, so it can appear under “Prerequisites” even though it’s not an additional requirement.   Sometimes SAP notes are updated with multiple correction instruction (CI) versions. Listing itself ensures the Note Assistant checks the correct version before implementation.  Check this SAP Help Identifying Prerequisite SAP Notes

entry.title: Password Reset / Could not decrypt token javax.crypto.AEADBadTagException: mac check in GCM failed
entry.content:
Dear Community,

When using the password reset OOTB functionality with Commerce Cloud version 2211.47 we receive following warning when submitting the changed password:

WARN  2026-03-06 12:25:03,835 [DefaultSecureTokenService] [hybrisHTTP31]  Could not decrypt token: javax.crypto.AEADBadTagException: mac check in GCM failed

We didn't customize anything with respect to encryption or decryption of the token link for the password reset email.

This occurs for us since version 2211.47 on all environments local, DEV, Staging and PROD CCv2.

 

I would be glad if you have a hint for us.

Thanks and best regards

Paul

SAP Commerce  SAP Commerce Cloud

entry.title: Time Account Type Validity in Successfactors
entry.content:
Hello Experts, 

I need your suggestion to map the below scenario for a new time type in Successfactors:

For a new time type effective from 01-April, I need to create a time account type. For 1st year, time account should be valid for 9 months i.e. from 01-April to 31-December and from next year onwards Time account type is valid for whole year i.e. from 01-January to 31-December. Accrual is annual for this time account. 

I am not able to see the option to map this scenario in time account type configuration. I also tried business rule as well but account validity date is always 1 year.

Please suggest. 

Thanks in advance. 

Thanks,

Hello,

Thank you for your question!

On March 4, I delivered the final session of the 8‑session series “Get Certified – SAP HCM Payroll for SAP S/4HANA.” I’m planning to repeat the series in early summer, and you’ll be able to register for it using the link below:

Get Certified: SAP Certified Associate - SAP HCM Payroll for SAP

At the end of last year, I also prepared and delivered two dedicated deep‑dive sessions on Managing and Processing Loans in SAP Payroll, based on a customer request.

If you have suggestions for other payroll topics you’d like to see covered, please feel free to share them here. If the topic is relevant to a broader group, we can certainly schedule an additional expert session.

You can also explore the upcoming live session schedule and register here:

Tatiana

entry.title: Re: SAP note required by itself? Question about SAP Suppo...
entry.content:
Thanks, you're right about the note being included itself in the hierarchy, in the program SCWN_PREREQUISITE_CALC_SWI:

If I display the note 3428373 via SNOTE, I can see that the correction instruction 1402901 is a manual activity of the note:

So, it says that the manual activity 1402901 is to be done before applying the correction instruction 1403042 via SNOTE. Only 1403042 is required because my system has the component S4CORE SP 108.

NB: by clicking on the Available View "Prequisite SAP Notes", the program SCWN_PREREQUISITE_CALC_SWI is started.

NB: in the SAP Support portal, the manual activity is shown in the main screen (without number), the automatic instructions are shown in a separate screen (with numbers more or less apparent):

Manual activity (number 1402901 not shown):

entry.title: Re: How to configure Single Sign-On (SSO) between S/4HANA...
entry.content:
Hi Khaing, for your SSO requirement between S/4HANA Cloud and BTP, I suggest exploring SAP Build Work Zone. It serves as a centralized entry point that can handle authentication seamlessly across both environments. 

Search for "SAP Build Work Zone" on developers.sap.com -  you'll find plenty of tutorials covering integration and SSO setup (specifically principal propagation) between S/4HANA and BTP. Good luck!

entry.title: Re: Ajuste de inventário com emissão de Nota Fiscal
entry.content: Bom dia! Sei que a thread tem mais de 10 anos, mas me deparei com este cenário recentemente em um projeto e estou com algumas dúvidas. No caso é possível gerar a nota automaticamente de forma standard após o uso da transação MI07 e a geração do documento de material? Quando realizo esse procedimento é informado o erro 8B262 - Nenhuma nota fiscal ligada ao documento. Realizei as configurações derivação do tipo de nota de nota fiscal na visão J_1BIM02V, mas ainda sim, a nota fiscal não é gerada. Se possível, poderia esclarecer o caso por favor?

entry.title: What’s New in Asset Management in SAP Cloud ERP Private 2025 FPS01
entry.content:
SAP Cloud ERP Private 2025 FPS01 release delivers a strong collection of enhancements across Asset Management, with improvements in maintenance execution, safety, planning, technical object management, and extensibility.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (the foundation of SAP Cloud ERP Private) strengthens efficient, data‑driven maintenance in this release while elevating user experience and process consistency across the entire asset lifecycle.

In this blog post, you’ll find a curated overview of the most important innovations, grouped by theme and written for maintenance technicians, planners, reliability engineers, and integration partners who rely on SAP to keep operations running safely and efficiently.

For a broader look at what’s new across other lines of business in the SAP Cloud ERP Private 2025 FPS01 release, don’t miss the blog by Yannick Peterschmitt: What’s New in the SAP Cloud ERP Private 2025 FPS1

Table of content:

  • Safety, compliance & field execution excellence
    - Isolation Management in Permit to Work – Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) 
    - Associated work permits in Manage Maintenance Orders app 
    - Inspection checklist enhancements 
    - After‑job recording for unplanned work 
    - New capabilities in Perform Maintenance Jobs app
  • Maintenance request & work pack enhancements
    - Improvements in Screen Maintenance Requests app 
    - Custom logic to add e‑mail recipients for job pack outputs
  • Planning and strategy enhancements
    - Additional functionality in Manage Maintenance Plans and Items apps 
    - Extended planning bucket capabilities 
    - Improvements for phase control code and analytics based on phases
  • Master data and technical object innovations
    - Reuse value help app for technical objects 
    - Hierarchy structure view for reference structures

  • SAP Asset Performance Management 
  • More information

Safety, compliance & field execution excellence

Isolation Management in Permit to Work – Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)

The 2025 FPS01 release introduces a fully embedded Isolation Management capability that brings the complete Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) lifecycle into SAP S/4HANA Asset Management. Through new configuration options and dedicated Fiori apps, maintenance organizations can structure and govern how isolation activities are defined, prepared, approved, and executed.

A new customizing section enables planning plants to specify isolation certificate behavior, certificate and template types, number ranges, and approval setups. The Manage Isolation Certificate Templates app (F8505) allows users to build reusable templates by adding technical objects, attaching work permit templates, assigning approvers, and adding supporting documents, ensuring consistent and compliant LOTO preparation.

Manage Isolation Certificate Templates app (F8505)

 

With the Manage Isolation Certificates app (F8504), users can create certificates from scratch or from a predefined template, add and process isolation items, move them through operational cycles such as tagging, testing, and untagging, assign work permits and approvals, and attach relevant documents. Printable outputs for certificates, lists, and tags support on‑site execution.

These innovations strengthen safety compliance and reduce operational risk through modernized digital isolation management, improves coordination between operations and maintenance to boost asset productivity, enhances the handling of technical objects during LOTO procedures, and increases overall usability of the permit‑to‑work process.

Check this Road Map item: Isolation management in permit to work (lockout tagout)

Associated work permits in Manage Maintenance Orders app

Work permits are now seamlessly integrated into the Manage Maintenance Orders (F4604) app, becoming available whenever a Permit‑to‑Work profile is maintained for the planning plant. A dedicated Work Permits section provides immediate visibility of permit details, including approval status, implemented precautions, certificate progress, and handout information.

Users can navigate directly to the related permit or create a new one linked to the order and planning plant. In addition, a Fiori‑based mass creation job enables work permits to be generated for multiple orders at once, using filtering criteria and template determination to streamline preparation and ensure consistent alignment with Work Clearance Management.

Read more about it: Mass Generation of Permit to Work Objects | SAP Help Portal

Work permits section in Manage Maintenance Orders (F4604) app

 

Inspection checklist enhancements

Inspection checklist processing receives several refinements that improve configuration, transparency, and usability across maintenance workflows. A new customizing knot centralizes all checklist‑related settings, creating a clearer foundation for managing current and future checklist configurations. Updated output forms now reflect material‑based checklists, covering serial‑number and component information in maintenance orders and work packs, ensuring documents accurately represent checklist content.

Enhancements in the Manage Inspection Checklists app (F6607) introduce richer detail in inspection lots, including quantities, reservation data, component references, serial numbers (if enabled), and new filtering options such as technical object and inspection lot status. Task lists now display a material checklist indicator, and the related API has been expanded to support reading, creating, and updating this field.

Within Manage Maintenance Orders app (F5241), checklist types are visible at operation level, cross‑app navigation to inspection checklists is supported, and actions such as “Generate Checklist” streamline preparation. Authorization checks for checklist types help ensure only permitted users can assign or modify certain checklist categories, providing stronger control over sensitive checklist usage.

Find more in the Road Map: SAP Road Map Explorer

Manage Inspection Checklists and Manage Maintenance Orders apps

After‑job recording for unplanned work

Technicians can now document maintenance activities that were performed immediately during an ad‑hoc repair, even when no prior order or confirmation existed. Using the new Confirmation of Unplanned Work processing context, they record failure data, measurements, consumed components, attachments, and time confirmations after completing the physical repair, ensuring proper traceability without delaying the actual fix.

A dedicated customizing knot controls default behaviors such as notification type, priority, and order type, and determines whether statuses like Work Completed or Job Finished are automatically applied. Authorization checks ensure only permitted users can create or submit unplanned work confirmations. Technicians can start the process either from the Perform Maintenance Jobs (F5104A) app, where the context is preselected, or from Create Maintenance Request (F1511A), where it must be chosen manually.

Once submitted, the system creates the necessary order operation (often directly in the MAIN execution stage) and enables technicians to capture all required details in a structured flow. Additional behavior, such as phase transitions or automatic technical completion, follows plant‑specific customizing settings.

Check more in the Road Map: SAP Road Map Explorer

Confirmation of Unplanned Work

 

 

New capabilities in Perform Maintenance Jobs app

The Perform Maintenance Jobs app (F5104A) receives numerous usability and functional enhancements that make job execution, time recording, and material handling more transparent and efficient. Key improvements include:

Configuration & time recording

  • New customizing knot for clearer setup of all F5104A‑related configuration steps.
  • Flexible defaulting of employee for time confirmations (logged‑in user, main technician, previous confirmation, or empty).
  • Introduction of Degree of Progress to capture physical work completion independently from actual work time, with UI slider and defaults based on last confirmation.

Perform Maintenance Jobs app (F5104A)

Job pausing & transparency

  • New pause codes for documenting why a job is paused, with optional or mandatory entry modes and full history tracking.

Notification processing

  • Long text templates can be applied during malfunction data capture, supporting guided, structured input and respecting notification type text rules.

Technical object visibility

  • New overview of object list entries with processing‑indicator editing (if authorized) and cross‑app navigation.

Material handling improvements

  • Transparency for EWM‑managed materials, automatically disabling Post/Return actions when required.
  • Better handling for batch‑managed materials, including mandatory batch entry and smart‑link navigation from material numbers.
  • Enhanced support for serialized materials, including value help and duplicate‑number checks during posting.

List page enhancements

  • A redesigned list table with more visible results, improved column management, context menu, and richer notification quick info.

Redesigned list table in Perform Maintenance Jobs app

Maintenance request & work pack enhancements

Improvements in Screen Maintenance Requests app

The Screen Maintenance Requests (F4072) Fiori app now adopts a flexible column layout so users can view the request list alongside its details in resizable, side‑by‑side columns, reducing back‑and‑forth navigation and accelerating review.

In addition, a new Change Scheduling action enables mass updates of open notifications for key scheduling fields (Priority, Maintenance Event, Required Start, Required Finish, and Final Due Date) directly from the list page; changes are captured in the History tab for traceability of updates to consequence and likelihood values.

Screen Maintenance Requests (F4072) app

 

Custom logic to add e‑mail recipients for job pack outputs

A new enhancement makes it easier to distribute job pack and work pack outputs to all relevant stakeholders. Using a dedicated BAdI, planners can now add additional e‑mail recipients to work pack output items, such as pick lists or instructions, through Output Parameter Determination. This logic can be activated for specific output types and e‑mail channels (To, Cc, Bcc), and supports flexible recipient management via custom tables, reusable elements, or SAP distribution lists.

At the same time, work pack creation becomes more versatile. The previous limitation restricting creation to execution or post‑execution phases is removed; planners can now enable work pack creation across the entire maintenance order lifecycle based on work pack type settings. New customizing options allow organizations to manage where restrictions apply for both phase and non‑phase‑enabled orders, increasing control and flexibility.

Custom logic to add e‑mail recipients for job pack outputs

Planning and strategy enhancements

Additional functionality in Manage Maintenance Plans and Items apps

Maintenance planners gain more flexibility and control through several usability improvements in the Manage Maintenance Plan (F5325) and Manage Maintenance Item (F5356) apps. The plan app now allows users to search maintenance plans directly by description, making it easier to identify and retrieve relevant plans.

In the maintenance item app, planners can filter items by maintenance plan status and display the plan status directly in the item list, offering clearer visibility into which items belong to which plan stage.

Manage Maintenance Plan (F5325) and Manage Maintenance Item (F5356) apps

Extended planning bucket capabilities

The planning bucket framework is expanded to give maintenance planners more control over how backlogs are grouped and prepared for execution. With this enhancement, users can define a broader and more flexible range of maintenance backlogs to be included in a reference or event-based planning bucket. This includes the ability to add additional grouping fields, enabling maintenance teams to organize work in ways that reflect real operational structures or business priorities.

For example, planners can now link an improvement project to a planning bucket, allowing all backlogs associated with that project to be bundled into a single, coherent scope. This creates a more holistic view of the work that needs to be scheduled and makes it easier to consolidate activities that naturally belong together.

These enhancements optimize the scope of maintenance backlogs planned within a given period, while giving planners the flexibility to reprioritize and adjust the bucket contents based on available capacity, budget, and strategic initiatives.

Improvements for phase control code and analytics based on phases

This enhancement strengthens the way maintenance organizations use the phase‑based maintenance model by improving both the usability of phase control codes and the analytics available around phase progression.

The definition of phase control codes has been simplified, so the same codes can be reused across different maintenance‑order types. This ensures more consistent behavior across teams and scenarios. The translation of both the phase control code and its description have been streamlined as well, making the codes easier to understand and apply in multi‑language environments.

In addition, new analytics allow planners to analyze the throughput of backlogs based on the average time each backlog item takes to move through the maintenance phases. This enables better visibility of bottlenecks and helps identify root causes behind slowdowns or delays. The result is clearer insights into phase progression and improved control of the maintenance workflow. 

Master data and technical object innovations

Reuse value help app for technical objects

Finding the right technical object, whether equipment or a functional location, often meant navigating different value help dialogs with varying layouts and search behaviours. To simplify this experience, a new unified value help has been introduced across Asset Management. This enhancement provides a single, consistent, and intuitive way to search for equipment, functional locations, and other technical objects in Fiori apps.

The new Technical Object Reuse Value Help is built on RAP‑based CDS views and delivers a more efficient, flexible search. Users can choose from multiple search templates, each surfacing different fields depending on their task, for example, focusing on labels, maintenance data, sales information, or address details.

This not only speeds up how users find the right object but also brings a more modern and consistent user experience across apps such as Find Technical Object (F2072) Manage Technical Object BOMs (F6070) and Manage Serial Numbers (F7617).

Behind the scenes, the value help objects for equipment, technical objects, and functional locations are fully reusable, so any Fiori app can adopt them without redesign, ensuring a harmonized look and feel across the maintenance process.

Technical Object Reuse Value Help

 

Hierarchy structure view for reference structures

Managing consistent and scalable asset structures becomes easier with two major innovations: Reference Equipment and Reference Structures. Together, they introduce a more controlled and harmonized approach to defining master data structures across plants and sites.

Reference equipment serves as a master template for creating and governing individual equipment records. It centralizes key information, such as general data, classification, measuring points, partner data, and change history, and provides a consistent basis for downstream equipment creation and updates through the Manage Reference Equipment app (F8304).

Reference structures provide a hierarchical view of reference functional locations and reference equipment, displayed in an intuitive split‑screen layout that combines structure navigation with detailed object information. Using the Manage Reference Structures apps (F8311/F8669), organizations can build and maintain standard structural templates and use them as the master for mass creation of individual structures.

Together, these capabilities help enforce consistent asset modeling across sites, enable mass changes, and reduce manual effort and errors.

Manage Reference Equipment app (F8304)

SAP Asset Performance Management 

SAP Asset Performance Management integrates with SAP Cloud ERP Private and empowers asset owners, plant managers, and reliability engineers to optimize maintenance strategies, measure and improve asset performance, and follow through on your sustainability objectives. The 2602 release includes improvements such as smarter notification replication between SAP ERP and APM, expanded industrial system modeling, extensible and time-aware FMEA assessments, and streamlined rule execution. Together, these updates help reduce manual effort while improving data clarity, structural transparency, and lifecycle control. Read the complete blog post by Kate Dowle: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2602.

If you’d like to stay up to date with the latest APM innovations, follow my colleague Kate Dowle who regularly shares updates and new blog posts on the topic.

Summary

With these delivered innovations, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition FPS01 significantly strengthens Asset Management capabilities, from daily technician execution to long‑term planning and technical object governance. Each enhancement contributes to safer operations, improved data consistency, and more efficient maintenance processes, supporting organizations on their path toward intelligent, connected, and resilient asset performance.

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Also, visit the Asset Management community page. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to find our latest blog posts. 

Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up-to-date to our latest enablement assets: Fernanda Rodrigues | LinkedIn

More information

  • What’s New Viewer
  • From Innovation to Impact: SAP Cloud ERP Private 2025 Release (SAP News Center)
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Community
  • Help Portal SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • What's New for Joule Capabilities
  • SAP Roadmap Explorer for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • SAP Business Suite Product Expert Training H1 2026

We at Cloud ERP Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: Re: Need to raise the priority of ticket
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

You can request raising the priority of your case through Customer Interaction Center (CIC) as described in the following KBA:

2682601 - How to raise the priority of an existing case to Very High by contacting the CIC team

Please do not open a new case for your request.

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: ‌🇩🇪‌ DSAG Jahreskongress 2026 (German SAP User Group Annual Conference)
entry.content:
We are inviting you to visit the DSAG Jahreskongress 2026, the leading annual conference for SAP user companies in German‑speaking countries. The event will take place in Cologne.

The DSAG annual congress is widely regarded as the most important meeting point for SAP customers in the region. It provides a structured setting for professional exchange, practical orientation, and networking among SAP users, partners, and SAP representatives.

Participants can expect a comprehensive program covering current developments in and around SAP. The agenda combines customer experience reports, expert contributions, and opportunities for direct dialogue, supporting informed discussion on both operational and strategic topics.

Localization topics play a key role throughout the event. This year, SAP Globalization will focus on the following areas:

  • e‑Invoicing
  • SAP Document and Reporting Compliance
  • Payroll Germany
More information is here.

For detailed information and updates on SAP Document and Reporting Compliance in Germany, join the Germany – SAP Document and Reporting Compliance Work Zone group.
